author: @kieronwheeler
text: Very neat idea, nice job! As it's consuming 16mA, is that per line or all together? I'm curious to know whether each line is 750ohms or all 8 lines, and interested in the relationship between width, number of coats and resistance. I would assume more coats decrease resistance and thinner lines increase resistance... ?? How controllable is this if you wanted to be very precise and make a component like this very small?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It's a homogeneous product so very precise - though resistance is going to be effected by environment so it would be a good idea to overcoat - maybe with a varnish or plastic glued on top - something to stop the moisture getting in.

author: @mattjdavis
text: Robert, perhaps I am getting lost. I thought you had a sustained higher density battery design and a roadmap for further. Having been burned in business Myself I can appreciate some of your struggles but did that original plan change?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: don't mate - it's just the folks involved - it amazes me how many people just want to try and make money from playing with the market rather than making a product - so getting someone on board who actually wants to make the battery is proving more difficult than I imagined

replies.author: @mattjdavis
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith my apologies if I came off as judgemental. I’ve been following you for a while and just been seeing so many tangential products your building that I was curious if that was still the plan. Would accept any remote help? I’d like to get involved in whatever way you need help if that is still your end goal, feel free to reach out to chat further.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @mattjdavis help is always welcome mate - what were you thinking? It might be better if you write to me at robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com - cheers

Thanks for your time and have a blessed day

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the basic idea is good - but like most of these things the devil is in the detail - essentially what you are talking about it a bipolar plate battery and yes the work in large scale if you can prevent the electrolyte from one cell mixing with the electrolyte of another cell and that is not so easy - but do-able

author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
text: Ah the old way we used to do it, so much more simple and logical than using MOSFITS. I wonder what do think the efficiency ratio will be? Clipping voltages create so much chatter, I worry about these frequencies causing havoc on sensitive equipment.   A great way to charge a  laptop off solar, Rob, who cares about loses with Solar, 24v down to 19, might actually be less, with smooth DC.  Inspirational, you've got us thinking!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate I think we over complicate stuff - somehow we seem to think the more complex something is the better - often that just isn't true - the question, I think, is what is appropriate for the job you need to do?

author: @Killianwsh
text: Your absolutely correct Rob! It most definitely is awesome! :-) Out of curiosity, do you think your rub on priming technique would work on polyolefin plastics like polypropylene? I've been tinkering with methods to raise the surface energy of  pp (home made corona field treatment built from an old "violet wand" skin therapy device) to increase bonding adhesion when using less costly adhesives.  PP is great stuff, inexpensive & very robust, but also a bit maddening to try to get anything to stick too!  Would love to hear any thoughts or tips you might have moment to share. (Or even possibly a quick video?...maybe:-) I'm sure I cant be the only one a bit confounded by this stuff! Lol :-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a really interesting question. mate - I will give it a go - tbh I suspect the answer is yes it will do it

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
This works for Robert's use case.  If you have resistive loads, the voltage can change  with the load - you can correct for this by measuring voltage with the loads connected or measuring resistance and using ohms law.  ... or use an appropriate zener diode.

Fun fact: It wasn't the Chinese who first started removing functionally unnecessary components (Muntzing).  Muntzing is named after Earl "madman" Muntz who did it with televisions and other electronic devices in the mid 1900s.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice add mate - thanks - and fun fact lol

author: @philipvernejules9926
text:
..........back when voltage dividers were more common in valve and solid state electronics , the rule of thumb was that the resistance of the tapping load  had to be at least ten times the resistance of  it's portion of the divider network. The old old TVs easily consumed 300 watts but it didn't matter in the profligate days .  There's more useage of high frequency conversion these days to enable the use of small ferrite transformers to solve voltage requirements..... For your heating pads I was just privately thinking that there may not be be any heating effect for areas spanned by the adhesive copper foil. In looks like it takes up say 20% of heater pad area. Could you perhaps have split the foil into thinner sections? .There can't be any heating effects where the copper foil is surely.....I'm probably still in the dark about what the silar device will be for but I know all will be revealed.... Oh,, I see nice symmetry,, you make a voltage divider,,well you could also make a voltage multiplier using a4 size bopet projector sheets , diodes, and painted graphene dielectric capacitors to power a Jacobs ladder maybe. AC is needed..
....very good resourcefulness on your part with the setup...

Translation----
Valve = tube as in thermionics
Solid state electronics = semiconductor electronics as was once the lexicon.

author: @imadjawad4408
text:
Thank you for such great demo for the product, would using a protective coat prevent ink resistance changes? What kind of coat? Is there a copper version of this interesting ink? Will I be able to spray it?

Thank you :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suppose so mate but it is carbon based and pretty stable - even so a coat of acrylic varnish wouldn't hurt it - it can be sprayed no problems - I used an airbrush in one video

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: since he says acrylic works I would think that polyurethane sealant might work too, and I have found it is pretty tough as a wire coating once it has enough time to fully cure, almost as good (toughness) as the coatings on "magnet" wire. :) (makes me wonder if a good tough epoxy might be good too, along with polystyrene peanuts dissolved in xylene to make styrene paint which I have done but I know styrene is kinda sensitive to UV and the like causing it to degrade so far less durable than the carbon I am guessing :) (but could be a better dielectric insulator, and then encase that under the urethane? anyway interesting ideas all around, cant wait to get time to do some experimenting XD
